heading: JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE
label: 28 JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE
notes: This title was enacted by ,act June 25, 1948, ch. 646, § 162 Stat. 869
Amendments1966—, , , substituted “Department of Justice” for “United States Attorneys and Marshals” in item for part II.Pub. L. 89–554, § 4(a)80 Stat. 611Sept. 6, 1966

Enactment Into Law; CitationSection 1 of , , provided in part:act June 25, 1948, ch. 64662 Stat. 869“That title 28 of the United States Code, entitled ‘Judicial Code and Judiciary’ is hereby revised, codified, and enacted into law, and may be cited as ‘Title 28, United States Code, section ___.’ ”

Legislative ConstructionSection 33 of , , provided that:act June 25, 1948, ch. 64662 Stat. 991“No inference of a legislative construction is to be drawn by reason of the chapter in Title 28, Judiciary and Judicial Procedure, as set out in section 1 of this Act, in which any section is placed, nor by reason of the catchlines used in such title.”

SeparabilitySection 34 of , , provided that:act June 25, 1948, ch. 64662 Stat. 991“If any part of Title 28, Judiciary and Judicial Procedure, as set out in section 1 of this Act, shall be held invalid, the remainder shall not be affected thereby.”
Effective DateSection 38 of , , provided that:act June 25, 1948, ch. 64662 Stat. 992“The provisions of this Act shall take effect on .”September 1, 1948

Repeals; Rights and Liabilities Saved, , repealed the sections or parts thereof of the Revised Statutes of the United States, Statutes at Large, or the Revised Statutes of the District of Columbia covering provisions codified in this title, but saved any rights or liabilities then existing under said sections or parts thereof.Act June 25, 1948, ch. 646, § 3962 Stat. 992R.S. § 1012 as affected by , [section 880 of former Title 28, Judicial Code and Judiciary], provided that appeals from district courts shall be subject to the same rules, regulations, and restrictions as are or may be prescribed in law in cases of writs of error. This provision was repealed by , . Section 2 of , , as amended , ; , [section 861b of former Title 28, Judicial Code and Judiciary], provided that: “All Acts of Congress referring to writs of error shall be construed as amended to the extent necessary to substitute appeal for writ of error.”act Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 29136 Stat. 1167act June 25, 1948, ch. 646, § 3962 Stat. 992act Jan. 31, 1928, ch. 1445 Stat. 54Apr. 26, 1928, ch. 44045 Stat. 466June 25, 1948, ch. 646, § 2362 Stat. 990

Writs of Error, [section 861a of former Title 28, Judicial Code and Judiciary], provided that: This provision was omitted from the 1948 Revised Judicial Code as obsolete, and repealed by , . Section 2 of act , which originally related to procedure in appeal cases, was also omitted from the 1948 Revised Judicial Code and was amended generally by , . Text as amended by section 23 of act , is set out as a note preceding , General Provisions.Act Jan. 31, 1928, ch. 14, § 145 Stat. 54act June 25, 1948, ch. 646, § 3962 Stat. 992act June 25, 1948, ch. 646, § 2362 Stat. 990section 1 of Title 1“The writ of error in cases, civil and criminal, is abolished. All relief which heretofore [] could be obtained by writ of error shall hereafter be obtainable by appeal.”Jan. 31, 1928Jan. 31, 1928June 25, 1948

Title 28 as Continuation of Existing Law; Change of Name of Circuit Courts of Appeals, , provided that:Act June 25, 1948, ch. 646, § 2(b)62 Stat. 985“The provisions of Title 28, Judiciary and Judicial Procedure, of the United States Code, set out in section 1 of this Act, with respect to the organization of each of the several courts therein provided for and of the Administrative Office of the United States Courts, shall be construed as continuations of existing law, and the tenure of the judges, officers, and employees thereof and of the United States attorneys and marshals and their deputies and assistants, in office on the effective date of this Act [], shall not be affected by its enactment, but each of them shall continue to serve in the same capacity under the appropriate provisions of title 28, as set out in section 1 of this Act, pursuant to his prior appointment: , That each circuit court of appeals shall, as in said title 28 set out, hereafter be known as a United States court of appeals. No loss of rights, interruption of jurisdiction, or prejudice to matters pending in any of such courts on the effective date of this Act shall result from its enactment.”Sept. 1, 1948Provided, however
